title: Compress this payroll-software market sizing to its investable thesis
guidance: Reward the version that connects the market number to an investment thesis. Penalise the version that merely summarises the data without a point of view.

parts.label: Market headline
parts.note: size + growth rate in one line

parts.label: Thesis bullets
parts.note: 3 bullets: why now, why here, what is the wedge

runner_input.instruction: Compress the market sizing below into the market section of an investment memo. Connect the numbers to a thesis.
runner_input.constraints: Max 220 words. State market size and growth. Explain why now and what the wedge is. Skip background and methodology.

source_blocks.text: UK SME payroll software market. Sizing based on ONS business count data: 1.4m businesses with 5–249 employees; approximately 60% (840k) currently using dedicated payroll software; the remaining 40% (560k) still using spreadsheets or accountant-managed payroll.

items: Current dedicated-payroll SAM: ~£1.1bn (840k firms x £1.3k avg ACV)
Greenfield opportunity (switchers from manual): ~£730m at same ACV
Market growing ~11% YoY driven by auto-enrolment complexity, IR35 enforcement, and RTI mandate upgrades
Incumbent weakness: two legacy players hold 55% share but have not shipped a major product update in 3+ years; NPS in customer surveys averages 18
New entrant tailwind: open banking (bank-feed payroll reconciliation) and HMRC API modernisation (2025 rollout) lower the build cost for challengers

rubric_notes: The investable thesis is: large SAM (£1.8bn), incumbents with low NPS and no recent innovation, and a structural tailwind from HMRC API modernisation that lowers build cost.
The wedge is open banking + HMRC API — not just market size.
Penalise responses that lead with the total SAM without connecting it to why now and what makes displacement achievable.
